D:\ACKIOS\toolkit\node_modules\flatted\package.json
{
  "name": "flatted",
  "version": "3.4.2",
  "description": "A super light and fast circular JSON parser.",
  "unpkg": "min.js",
  "main": "./cjs/index.js",
  "scripts": {
    "build": "npm run cjs && npm run rollup:esm && npm run rollup:es && npm run rollup:babel && npm run min && npm run test && npm run size",
    "cjs": "ascjs esm cjs",
    "rollup:es": "rollup --config rollup/es.config.js && sed -i.bck 's/^var /self./' es.js && rm -rf es.js.bck",
    "rollup:esm": "rollup --config rollup/esm.config.js",
    "rollup:babel": "rollup --config rollup/babel.config.js && sed -i.bck 's/^var /self./' index.js && rm -rf index.js.bck",
    "min": "terser index.js -c -m -o min.js",
    "size": "cat index.js | wc -c;cat min.js | wc -c;gzip -c9 min.js | wc -c;cat min.js | brotli | wc -c; cat es.js | brotli | wc -c; cat esm.js | brotli | wc -c",
    "test": "c8 node test/index.js",
    "test:php": "php php/test.php",

D:\ACKIOS\toolkit\.private\architecture\50_STANDARDS\ACKIOS_MATURITY_MODEL.md
# ACKIOS Maturity Model (AMM)

## Purpose

Defines the maturity levels used to evaluate every ACKIOS Core.

------------------------------------------------------------

Level 0 — Not Started

No architecture or implementation exists.

------------------------------------------------------------

Level 1 — Defined

Architecture, contracts and policies exist.

------------------------------------------------------------

Level 2 — Implemented

Core functionality is implemented.

------------------------------------------------------------

Level 3 — Integrated

Integrated with other Core components.

------------------------------------------------------------

Level 4 — Verified

Protected by tests, audits and validation.

------------------------------------------------------------

Level 5 — Certified

Approved for Release and used in production workflow.

------------------------------------------------------------

Every Core is evaluated using this model.

D:\ACKIOS\toolkit\.private\architecture\ACKIOS_ARCHITECTURE_CONSTITUTION.md
# ACKIOS Architecture Constitution

## Mission

This document defines the immutable architectural principles of ACKIOS.

All future development must comply with these principles.

------------------------------------------------------------

# Principle 1

Architecture before Implementation

Architecture is designed before implementation begins.

------------------------------------------------------------

# Principle 2

Policy before Automation

Automation implements policies.

Automation never defines policies.

------------------------------------------------------------

# Principle 3

Single Source of Truth

Every architectural concept has exactly one authoritative source.

------------------------------------------------------------

# Principle 4

Repository Boundary

Three repository zones exist:

PUBLIC

WORK

PRIVATE

Their responsibilities never overlap.

------------------------------------------------------------

# Principle 5

Public Repository

main represents only public product artifacts.

main is never used as a development workspace.

------------------------------------------------------------

# Principle 6

Core Independence

Every Core exposes public contracts.

Internal implementation is never accessed directly.

------------------------------------------------------------

# Principle 7

Incremental Evolution

ACKIOS evolves through:

Story

v

Epic

v

Core

Large rewrites are avoided.

------------------------------------------------------------

# Principle 8

Automation protects Architecture

DevOS exists to enforce architecture.

Automation must reduce architectural risk.

------------------------------------------------------------

# Principle 9

Security before Convenience

Protection of strategic knowledge always has higher priority than development convenience.

------------------------------------------------------------

# Principle 10

Release Governance

Publication is allowed only after all architectural, quality and repository checks have passed.

D:\ACKIOS\toolkit\.private\architecture\DEVOS_CORE_ARCHITECTURE.md
# DevOS Core Architecture

## Purpose

Defines the standard architecture for every DevOS Core.

---

# Layer Model

Every Core follows the same dependency model.

Contracts
    v
Models
    v
Services
    v
Commands

Dependencies are one-directional only.

---

# Contracts

Describe public result objects and interfaces.

Examples:

- WorkflowState
- RepositoryBoundaryResult
- ReleaseResult
- KnowledgeResult

---

# Models

Domain entities and internal data structures.

---

# Services

Business logic.

Services may use Contracts and Models.

Services never call Commands.

---

# Commands

User entry points.

Examples:

- START_CHAT
- END_CHAT
- STATUS
- Publish-Main

Commands orchestrate Services.

---

# Core Structure

Workflow Core

Repository Governance Core

Knowledge Core

Planning Core

Release Core

Automation Core

All future cores follow the same architecture.

---

# Repository Rule

No Core may bypass another Core's public contract.

Communication happens through Contracts and Services only.


########################################
D:\ACKIOS\toolkit\.private\architecture\REPOSITORY_BOUNDARY_POLICY.md
# ACKIOS Repository Boundary Policy

## Purpose

Defines repository publication rules for ACKIOS.

---

## Repository Zones

### PUBLIC

Allowed to be published.

### PRIVATE

Never published.

### WORK

Development workspace.
Never published.

---

## Publication Rule

main contains only public product artifacts.

.private is never published.

.work is never published.

---

## Release Rule

Updating main is allowed only after:

- Build PASS
- Tests PASS
- Architecture Health Check PASS
- Repository Boundary Check PASS
- Epic completed
- Release review approved

D:\ACKIOS\toolkit\.private\ACKIOS_MASTER_VISION.md
# ACKIOS Master Vision

## 1. Founder Vision

ACKIOS is created with the goal of building a new generation software intelligence platform.

The mission is to create a system capable of understanding, analyzing, and helping evolve complex digital systems.

ACKIOS should become more than a development tool.

It should become an intelligence layer between humans and software complexity.

---

## 2. The Problem ACKIOS Solves

Modern software systems are becoming increasingly complex.

Developers face challenges:

- understanding large architectures;
- maintaining knowledge;
- managing dependencies;
- predicting consequences of changes;
- preserving system understanding over time.

ACKIOS exists to reduce this complexity through intelligence.

---

## 3. New Technology Category

The long-term objective is to create a new category:

Software Evolution Intelligence Platform.

This category combines:

- architecture intelligence;
- knowledge systems;
- cognitive processing;
- reasoning;
- intelligent agents;
- continuous evolution.

---

## 4. ACKIOS Intelligence Layer

ACKIOS should become the understanding layer of software systems.

It should help humans answer:

- What exists?
- How does it work?
- Why was it designed this way?
- What should improve?
- What will happen after changes?


---

## 5. Competitive Advantage

The competitive advantage of ACKIOS is not a single feature.

The advantage is the combination of multiple intelligence layers working together.

ACKIOS combines:

- architecture understanding;
- knowledge representation;
- persistent memory;
- cognitive processing;
- reasoning capabilities;
- intelligent agents.

Individual features can be reproduced.

However, the complete intelligence ecosystem requires years of accumulated architecture, knowledge, and evolution.

The uniqueness of ACKIOS comes from the integration of all intelligence layers into one platform.

---

## 6. Global Market Vision

The long-term vision is to position ACKIOS as a global software intelligence infrastructure platform.

Future software systems will become increasingly complex.

Organizations will need systems that can:

- understand existing software;
- preserve engineering knowledge;
- predict consequences;
- support decisions;
- accelerate evolution.

ACKIOS aims to become the intelligence layer that helps organizations manage software complexity.

---

## 7. Long Term Mission

The mission of ACKIOS is to help humanity create and evolve software systems through deeper understanding and intelligent collaboration.

ACKIOS should enable:

- better architecture;
- safer changes;
- preserved knowledge;
- faster innovation;
- continuous improvement.

---

## 8. Owner Principles

ACKIOS development should follow fundamental principles:

- protect long-term vision;
- build unique technology;
- preserve architectural integrity;
- prioritize understanding over automation;
- grow carefully without losing differentiation.

The goal is not only to build software.

The goal is to create a lasting intelligence platform for the future of software engineering.

D:\ACKIOS\toolkit\.private\BRAIN_INTERNAL_DESIGN.md
# ACKIOS Brain Internal Design

## 1. Brain Philosophy

The ACKIOS Brain is the central intelligence concept of the platform.

The Brain is not designed as a simple assistant.

It is designed as an internal intelligence framework capable of understanding software systems, maintaining knowledge, supporting reasoning, and enabling intelligent evolution.

The purpose of the Brain is to transform ACKIOS from a development toolkit into an intelligent software partner.

---

## 2. Brain Kernel Model

The Brain Kernel is the foundation of ACKIOS intelligence.

It connects:

- knowledge;
- memory;
- context;
- reasoning;
- cognitive processes;
- agents.

The Brain Kernel coordinates intelligence capabilities while preserving architectural boundaries.

---

## 3. Brain Design Principles

The Brain follows several principles:

- understanding before action;
- knowledge before automation;
- context before decisions;
- explainability before execution;
- controlled evolution before uncontrolled growth.

The Brain should increase human capability, not replace human judgment.


---

## 4. Memory Architecture

Memory is a fundamental intelligence capability of ACKIOS.

The purpose of memory is to preserve understanding across time.

ACKIOS should not analyze projects as temporary sessions.

It should maintain continuous knowledge about system evolution.

---

## Memory Layers

The ACKIOS memory architecture consists of multiple layers.

---

## Project Memory

Project Memory stores information about the current software system.

It includes:

- project structure;
- components;
- dependencies;
- configurations;
- current state.

---

## Architectural Memory

Architectural Memory preserves system design knowledge.

It includes:

- architectural decisions;
- boundaries;
- patterns;
- design principles;
- evolution history.

---

## Decision Memory

Decision Memory records important reasoning history.

It preserves:

- why decisions were made;
- alternatives considered;
- consequences;
- future references.

---

## Knowledge History

Knowledge History represents the continuous evolution of system understanding.

Each analysis cycle should increase ACKIOS knowledge about the project.

---

## Memory Principle

Memory transforms ACKIOS from a stateless tool into a continuously learning intelligence system.

The ability to remember is one of the foundations of future ACKIOS intelligence.


---

## 5. Knowledge Processing Architecture

The Knowledge Processing system transforms raw project information into structured intelligence.

The goal is not only to collect information.

The goal is to create meaningful understanding.

---

## Knowledge Processing Pipeline

The pipeline consists of several stages.

---

## Stage 1 — Information Collection

ACKIOS collects information from:

- source code;
- architecture models;
- dependencies;
- documentation;
- project history.

---

## Stage 2 — Knowledge Extraction

The system identifies:

- components;
- relationships;
- patterns;
- dependencies;
- architectural concepts.

---

## Stage 3 — Knowledge Modeling

Extracted information is transformed into structured knowledge.

The model represents:

- entities;
- relationships;
- properties;
- historical context.

---

## Stage 4 — Relationship Understanding

ACKIOS analyzes connections between system elements.

Examples:

- dependency relationships;
- architectural boundaries;
- behavioral patterns;
- change impact.

---

## Stage 5 — Intelligence Preparation

Processed knowledge becomes available for:

- reasoning;
- recommendations;
- planning;
- agent operations.

---

## Knowledge Processing Principle

Knowledge is not a static database.

It is an evolving intelligence model that becomes more valuable as ACKIOS continues to understand the system.


---

## 6. Context System Architecture

The Context System provides ACKIOS with awareness of the current situation.

Context allows the Brain to understand not only information, but also meaning.

---

## Context Sources

The Context System combines multiple sources:

- current project state;
- user objectives;
- historical knowledge;
- architectural memory;
- previous decisions;
- active tasks.

---

## Project Context

Project Context represents the current condition of the software system.

It includes:

- architecture state;
- active components;
- dependencies;
- detected issues;
- ongoing changes.

---

## User Context

User Context represents human objectives and intentions.

It includes:

- current goals;
- requested actions;
- priorities;
- expected outcomes.

---

## Historical Context

Historical Context provides continuity.

It allows ACKIOS to understand:

- previous decisions;
- past changes;
- evolution patterns;
- accumulated knowledge.

---

## Context Integration

The Brain combines different context sources into a unified understanding model.

This unified context becomes the foundation for:

- reasoning;
- recommendations;
- planning;
- intelligent assistance.

---

## Context Principle

Without context, intelligence becomes isolated processing.

With context, ACKIOS can understand situations and provide meaningful assistance.


---

## 7. Reasoning Pipeline

The Reasoning Pipeline transforms knowledge and context into intelligent conclusions.

Reasoning is the capability that allows ACKIOS to move beyond analysis.

---

## Reasoning Flow

The reasoning process consists of several stages.

---

## Stage 1 — Knowledge Input

The system receives information from:

- Knowledge Graph;
- Memory System;
- Project Context;
- Historical Data.

---

## Stage 2 — Context Understanding

ACKIOS evaluates information according to the current situation.

The system considers:

- project state;
- user objectives;
- previous decisions;
- possible consequences.

---

## Stage 3 — Problem Analysis

The system identifies:

- problems;
- opportunities;
- risks;
- dependencies;
- possible improvements.

---

## Stage 4 — Hypothesis Generation

ACKIOS creates possible interpretations and solutions.

Each hypothesis should be based on available knowledge and context.

---

## Stage 5 — Evaluation

Possible solutions are evaluated by:

- impact;
- risks;
- compatibility;
- architectural principles.

---

## Stage 6 — Decision Support

ACKIOS provides:

- recommendations;
- explanations;
- possible actions;
- expected outcomes.

---

## Reasoning Principle

ACKIOS reasoning must be explainable.

The system should not only provide answers.

It should provide understanding of how conclusions were reached.


---

## 8. Learning Mechanism

The Learning Mechanism enables ACKIOS to improve through accumulated experience.

Learning does not mean uncontrolled modification.

Learning means improving understanding through knowledge evolution.

---

## Learning Sources

ACKIOS can learn from:

- project evolution;
- architectural changes;
- previous recommendations;
- user feedback;
- system outcomes;
- discovered patterns.

---

## Learning Process

The learning cycle consists of:

---

## Stage 1 — Observation

ACKIOS observes:

- system changes;
- decisions;
- results;
- environmental conditions.

---

## Stage 2 — Pattern Discovery

The system identifies:

- repeated behaviors;
- successful approaches;
- architectural patterns;
- potential improvements.

---

## Stage 3 — Knowledge Update

New understanding is integrated into:

- Knowledge Graph;
- Memory System;
- Architectural Models.

---

## Stage 4 — Intelligence Improvement

Accumulated knowledge improves:

- recommendations;
- analysis quality;
- reasoning accuracy;
- future decisions.

---

## Learning Principle

ACKIOS should evolve through understanding.

The goal is not self-modifying code.

The goal is continuous improvement of intelligence and assistance quality.


---

## 9. Reflection System

The Reflection System allows ACKIOS to evaluate its own processes and improve intelligence quality.

Reflection provides the ability to analyze not only the system being studied, but also the quality of ACKIOS own results.

---

## Reflection Responsibilities

The Reflection System evaluates:

- analysis quality;
- recommendation effectiveness;
- reasoning accuracy;
- detected mistakes;
- improvement opportunities.

---

## Reflection Process

The reflection cycle includes:

---

## Stage 1 — Result Evaluation

ACKIOS reviews previous outputs.

It analyzes:

- generated recommendations;
- performed analysis;
- detected patterns;
- user responses.

---

## Stage 2 — Quality Assessment

The system evaluates:

- correctness;
- usefulness;
- reliability;
- architectural consistency.

---

## Stage 3 — Improvement Discovery

Reflection identifies:

- weak points;
- missing knowledge;
- possible improvements;
- better approaches.

---

## Stage 4 — Knowledge Refinement

Results of reflection improve:

- Memory System;
- Knowledge Graph;
- Reasoning Models;
- Future Recommendations.

---

## Reflection Principle

Reflection allows ACKIOS to become better over time.

The system should continuously improve the quality of understanding while preserving explainability and control.


---

## 10. Future Cognitive Evolution

The long-term evolution of ACKIOS is the transition from intelligent assistance toward software evolution intelligence.

The goal is to create a system that continuously improves understanding of complex digital systems.

---

## Evolution Direction

ACKIOS should evolve through several stages:

---

## Stage 1 — Understanding

The system understands:

- software structure;
- architecture;
- dependencies;
- relationships.

---

## Stage 2 — Intelligence

The system develops:

- knowledge models;
- reasoning capabilities;
- contextual awareness;
- intelligent recommendations.

---

## Stage 3 — Collaboration

The system enables:

- human-AI cooperation;
- specialized agents;
- intelligent workflows;
- engineering assistance.

---

## Stage 4 — Evolution Intelligence

The final objective is a platform capable of helping software systems evolve safely.

Capabilities include:

- continuous understanding;
- improvement discovery;
- architectural guidance;
- intelligent evolution planning.

---

## Cognitive Evolution Principle

ACKIOS must evolve responsibly.

Future intelligence capabilities must preserve:

- explainability;
- human control;
- architectural integrity;
- knowledge ownership.

---

## Final Brain Vision

The ACKIOS Brain represents the foundation of a new generation of software intelligence.

It transforms software projects from passive collections of files into understandable, evolving digital systems.

# ACKIOS Future Roadmap

## Epoch 1 — Foundation

Current Stage:

ACKIOS establishes the fundamental platform layer.

Main capabilities:

- architecture analysis;
- project understanding;
- dependency intelligence;
- architecture validation;
- developer tooling;
- plugin foundation.

The objective is to create a stable base for future intelligence capabilities.

---

## Epoch 2 — Intelligence Layer

Goal:

Transform ACKIOS from analysis tooling into a system that understands software projects.

Capabilities:

- Knowledge Graph;
- project memory;
- architecture intelligence;
- intelligent recommendations;
- deeper system understanding.

ACKIOS begins building a persistent model of software systems.


---

## Epoch 3 — ACKIOS Studio

Goal:

Create a visual intelligence environment for understanding and evolving software systems.

Capabilities:

- visual Architecture Map;
- interactive system exploration;
- Brain visualization;
- project intelligence dashboards;
- human-AI collaboration workflows.

ACKIOS Studio becomes the interface where humans interact with system intelligence.

---

## Epoch 4 — Autonomous Engineering

Goal:

Enable intelligent engineering assistance through specialized agents.

Capabilities:

- architecture agents;
- development agents;
- documentation agents;
- security agents;
- planning systems;
- reasoning workflows;
- controlled autonomous improvements.

Agents operate through Brain Kernel and Cognitive Runtime capabilities.

---

## Epoch 5 — Global Software Intelligence Platform

Goal:

Create a new category of software intelligence infrastructure.

Capabilities:

- enterprise-scale system understanding;
- continuous software evolution;
- advanced agent ecosystems;
- intelligent engineering environments;
- long-term software knowledge preservation.

ACKIOS becomes a platform for understanding, improving, and evolving complex digital systems.


---

## Long Term Vision

ACKIOS aims to create a new category of intelligent software evolution platforms.

The long-term vision is to build a system that understands software projects as evolving digital organisms.

---

## Future Capability Model

ACKIOS should combine:

- architecture intelligence;
- persistent knowledge;
- cognitive processing;
- reasoning systems;
- autonomous agents;
- continuous evolution.

---

## Strategic Goal

The ultimate goal is not to replace developers.

The goal is to create an intelligent engineering partner that helps humans understand, improve, and evolve complex software systems.

---

## Ownership Principle

The accumulated intelligence, architecture knowledge, and strategic evolution model represent core project value.

ACKIOS development should preserve technological uniqueness while allowing controlled ecosystem growth.

---

## Final Vision

ACKIOS becomes the intelligence layer between humans and complex software systems.

It transforms software development from manual management of complexity into a process of understanding, collaboration, and continuous evolution.

D:\ACKIOS\toolkit\.private\IP_STRATEGY.md
# ACKIOS IP Strategy

## 1. Intellectual Property Philosophy

The primary value of ACKIOS is not only source code.

The most important assets are:

- architecture knowledge;
- system design principles;
- intelligence models;
- cognitive concepts;
- evolution strategy;
- accumulated understanding.

ACKIOS must protect the ideas and structures that create long-term uniqueness.

---

## 2. Protected Assets

The following areas represent protected intellectual property:

- Private Core architecture;
- Brain Kernel concepts;
- Cognitive Runtime design;
- knowledge intelligence models;
- reasoning mechanisms;
- agent orchestration strategies;
- future platform evolution concepts.

These elements define the strategic advantage of ACKIOS.

---

## 3. Open Source Boundary

The Open Foundation exists to encourage:

- adoption;
- ecosystem growth;
- collaboration;
- developer participation.

Public components may include:

- developer tools;
- integrations;
- interfaces;
- extensions;
- general utilities.

The Open Foundation should demonstrate capability without exposing strategic intelligence layers.

---

## 4. Private Core Protection

The Private Core remains controlled by the project owner.

Protected areas include:

- internal intelligence architecture;
- proprietary knowledge models;
- advanced reasoning systems;
- future autonomous capabilities;
- strategic development plans.

The separation between public and private layers is a fundamental ACKIOS principle.

---

## 5. Collaboration Model

External collaboration should expand ACKIOS capabilities without exposing protected intelligence.

Collaboration principles:

- clear access boundaries;
- controlled contributions;
- documented ownership;
- protection of strategic knowledge.

The ecosystem may grow while preserving the uniqueness of ACKIOS.

---

## 6. Long Term Ownership Strategy

The long-term objective is to build sustainable technological value.

ACKIOS ownership includes:

- accumulated knowledge;
- architectural decisions;
- intelligence evolution;
- strategic direction.

The project should remain capable of developing unique technology while allowing controlled ecosystem participation.

---

## Final IP Principle

ACKIOS protects not only code.

ACKIOS protects the accumulated intelligence required to create the future software evolution platform.

# ACKIOS Owner Development Rules

## 1. Core Ownership Principle

ACKIOS is developed as a long-term technology platform.

The project value is based on:

- source code;
- architecture knowledge;
- intelligence concepts;
- accumulated decisions;
- future evolution strategy.

The owner must preserve the uniqueness and direction of ACKIOS.

---

## 2. Open Foundation Rules

The Open Foundation exists for:

- adoption;
- ecosystem growth;
- developer participation;
- community contribution.

Public development may include:

- tools;
- interfaces;
- integrations;
- plugins;
- general functionality.

Public components should demonstrate capability while protecting strategic intelligence.

---

## 3. Private Core Rules

Private Core contains the strategic intelligence layer.

Protected areas include:

- Brain architecture;
- cognitive systems;
- internal intelligence models;
- advanced reasoning mechanisms;
- future autonomous capabilities;
- strategic roadmap.

Private Core remains controlled by the owner.

---

## 4. Architecture Change Rules

Major architectural changes must preserve:

- layer separation;
- system integrity;
- long-term vision;
- maintainability.

New features should strengthen the overall intelligence platform.

---

## 5. Collaboration Rules

External collaboration should increase ecosystem value.

Collaboration must follow:

- clear boundaries;
- documented ownership;
- controlled access;
- architectural discipline.

Contributors expand ACKIOS without defining the strategic direction.

---

## 6. Documentation Rules

Important decisions must be documented.

Documentation preserves:

- architectural knowledge;
- evolution history;
- design reasoning;
- future understanding.

Knowledge is a core project asset.

---

## 7. Release Strategy

Releases should follow controlled evolution.

Priority order:

1. Stable foundation.
2. Intelligence capabilities.
3. User experience.
4. Ecosystem growth.
5. Advanced autonomous features.

Quality and direction are more important than speed.

---

## 8. Long Term Protection Rules

ACKIOS must protect:

- unique ideas;
- strategic architecture;
- intelligence concepts;
- accumulated knowledge.

The project should grow openly while preserving the core technological advantage.

---

## Final Principle

Build openly where it creates value.

Protect carefully where it creates uniqueness.

ACKIOS must evolve as a platform, not only as a product.

D:\ACKIOS\toolkit\.private\OWNER_SECRET_VISION.md
# ACKIOS Owner Secret Vision

## 1. Founder Intent

ACKIOS is not created as a simple development tool.

The purpose of ACKIOS is to become an intelligent software evolution platform capable of understanding, analyzing, improving, and assisting the creation of complex digital systems.

ACKIOS should evolve from a toolkit into an intelligent development ecosystem where software projects become understandable entities with their own architecture model, knowledge representation, memory, reasoning capabilities, and autonomous assistance.

The founder's intent is to build a unique technology platform that combines:

- software architecture intelligence;
- project understanding;
- knowledge graphs;
- cognitive processing;
- intelligent recommendations;
- autonomous agents;
- continuous system evolution.

ACKIOS must be developed as a long-term technology vision, not as a short-term application.

---

## 2. The Problem ACKIOS Solves

Modern software systems are becoming increasingly complex.

Developers struggle with:

- understanding large codebases;
- maintaining architecture consistency;
- discovering hidden dependencies;
- making safe changes;
- transferring knowledge between teams;
- preserving design decisions over time.

Traditional development tools mostly operate on files, commands, and isolated metrics.

ACKIOS aims to understand software systems as living structures.

---

## 3. The Ultimate Vision

The ultimate vision of ACKIOS is to create a new category:

An Intelligent Software Development Environment.

A system that can:

- understand a project;
- build a mental model of the system;
- analyze architecture;
- remember historical decisions;
- reason about possible changes;
- recommend improvements;
- assist developers;
- support autonomous engineering workflows.

ACKIOS should become a bridge between human creativity and machine intelligence.

---

## 4. ACKIOS as a New Technology Category

ACKIOS should not compete as another ordinary developer tool.

The goal is to create a new category of intelligent software infrastructure.

The category is:

Intelligent Software Evolution Platform.

This means ACKIOS should understand software systems beyond source code.

A traditional tool sees:

- files;
- folders;
- syntax;
- dependencies;
- commands.

ACKIOS should understand:

- architecture intent;
- relationships between components;
- historical decisions;
- system behavior;
- knowledge structures;
- possible future evolution paths.

ACKIOS should become a system that helps software projects evolve intelligently.

---

## 5. Unique Competitive Advantage

The core advantage of ACKIOS is not a single feature.

The advantage is the combination of multiple intelligence layers:

- Architecture Intelligence;
- Knowledge Graph;
- Cognitive Runtime;
- Memory System;
- Reasoning System;
- Planning System;
- Agent Ecosystem.

The long-term value comes from creating a persistent understanding layer between humans and complex software systems.

A competitor may reproduce individual features.

However, reproducing the complete integrated intelligence architecture requires the combination of:

- architecture understanding;
- project memory;
- reasoning capabilities;
- autonomous assistance;
- continuous evolution mechanisms.

ACKIOS must protect and develop this integrated vision.

---

## 6. Private Core Boundary

ACKIOS follows a two-layer development philosophy:

Open Foundation + Private Core.

The purpose of this separation is to allow ecosystem growth while protecting the most valuable intellectual assets.

---

## Open Foundation

The public layer may include:

- basic developer tooling;
- CLI capabilities;
- architecture scanning;
- reporting;
- plugin interfaces;
- documentation;
- community extensions.

The Open Foundation exists to create adoption, trust, and ecosystem growth.

---

## Private Core

The Private Core contains the strategic intelligence layer of ACKIOS.

The following areas are considered protected:

- advanced Brain architecture;
- cognitive processing mechanisms;
- internal reasoning systems;
- proprietary knowledge structures;
- advanced agent orchestration;
- future autonomous capabilities;
- strategic product evolution concepts.

The Private Core represents the long-term competitive advantage of ACKIOS.

---

## Protection Principle

ACKIOS should not protect only source code.

The most valuable asset is the accumulated architecture knowledge, system design decisions, intelligence models, and future evolution strategy.

The goal is to protect the ability of ACKIOS to become a unique intelligent software evolution platform.

---

## Development Rule

Public features may demonstrate capability.

Private systems create differentiation.

The separation between Open Foundation and Private Core must remain a fundamental architectural principle.

---

## 7. Future Intelligence Architecture

ACKIOS is designed to evolve through multiple intelligence layers.

The future architecture is not based only on automation.

It is based on understanding, memory, reasoning, and controlled evolution.

---

## Architecture Intelligence Layer

The first intelligence layer provides deep understanding of software systems.

Capabilities:

- architecture discovery;
- dependency understanding;
- structural analysis;
- system mapping;
- architectural recommendations.

This layer creates the foundation for software awareness.

---

## Knowledge Graph Layer

ACKIOS should maintain a connected representation of software knowledge.

The Knowledge Graph should represent:

- components;
- dependencies;
- relationships;
- decisions;
- historical changes;
- architectural knowledge.

The goal is to transform a codebase into an understandable knowledge model.

---

## Brain Kernel

The Brain Kernel represents the central intelligence layer.

Its responsibilities include:

- knowledge processing;
- context management;
- reasoning support;
- memory integration;
- intelligent analysis.

The Brain Kernel should become the foundation for advanced ACKIOS capabilities.

---

## Cognitive Runtime

The Cognitive Runtime provides higher-level intelligence processes:

- reasoning;
- planning;
- reflection;
- evaluation;
- decision support.

It allows ACKIOS to move from passive analysis toward intelligent assistance.

---

## Agent Ecosystem

Future ACKIOS agents should operate as specialized intelligence workers.

Examples:

- architecture agent;
- analysis agent;
- improvement agent;
- documentation agent;
- security agent.

Agents should work through controlled permissions and shared knowledge.

---

## Evolution Engine

The long-term goal is a system capable of continuous improvement.

ACKIOS should help projects:

- understand their current state;
- identify weaknesses;
- propose improvements;
- evolve safely.

The Evolution Engine represents the highest level of ACKIOS intelligence.

---

## 8. Long-Term Evolution Roadmap

ACKIOS development follows a long-term evolution strategy.

The project should evolve through several major epochs.

---

## Epoch 1 — Foundation

Goal:

Create a stable intelligent development foundation.

Core capabilities:

- CLI toolkit;
- architecture analysis;
- dependency understanding;
- reporting system;
- plugin foundation.

The objective is to create the base platform.

---

## Epoch 2 — Intelligence Layer

Goal:

Move from analysis toward understanding.

Capabilities:

- knowledge graph;
- project memory;
- architecture intelligence;
- recommendations;
- deeper system understanding.

ACKIOS begins forming a persistent model of software systems.

---

## Epoch 3 — ACKIOS Studio

Goal:

Create a visual intelligence environment.

Capabilities:

- visual architecture maps;
- interactive system exploration;
- Brain visualization;
- project intelligence dashboards;
- human-AI collaboration workflows.

---

## Epoch 4 — Autonomous Engineering

Goal:

Enable intelligent assistance through agents.

Capabilities:

- specialized engineering agents;
- planning systems;
- reasoning workflows;
- controlled autonomous improvements;
- intelligent development assistance.

---

## Epoch 5 — Global Intelligence Platform

Goal:

Create a new category of software intelligence infrastructure.

Capabilities:

- enterprise-scale understanding;
- continuous software evolution;
- advanced agent ecosystems;
- intelligent engineering environments.

ACKIOS becomes a platform for understanding and evolving complex digital systems.

---

## 9. Protected Knowledge

The most valuable asset of ACKIOS is not only the source code.

The primary asset is accumulated intelligence.

Protected knowledge includes:

- architectural decisions;
- system evolution strategies;
- internal intelligence models;
- cognitive system designs;
- knowledge representation methods;
- agent coordination approaches;
- future platform concepts.

The value of ACKIOS grows through accumulated understanding.

Every architectural decision, every learned relationship, and every intelligence capability increases the uniqueness of the system.

Protection of knowledge is protection of the future capability of ACKIOS.

---

## Knowledge Ownership Principle

ACKIOS knowledge must remain controlled by the project owner.

External collaboration should expand the ecosystem without exposing the strategic intelligence layer.

The goal is to create sustainable technological value while preserving the uniqueness of ACKIOS.

---

## 10. Founder Principles

ACKIOS development must follow several permanent principles.

---

## Principle 1 — Build Understanding Before Automation

ACKIOS should not automate blindly.

The system must first understand:

- architecture;
- context;
- relationships;
- consequences.

Intelligence begins with understanding.

---

## Principle 2 — Preserve Long-Term Vision

Short-term features must not destroy long-term architecture.

Every major decision should support the evolution toward an intelligent software platform.

---

## Principle 3 — Protect Core Differentiation

Open development should create ecosystem growth.

Private intelligence should preserve strategic uniqueness.

---

## Principle 4 — Quality Over Speed

ACKIOS should evolve carefully.

Architectural integrity is more important than rapid feature accumulation.

---

## Principle 5 — Human and Artificial Intelligence Partnership

ACKIOS should amplify human creativity.

The goal is not to replace engineers.

The goal is to create a new form of collaboration between humans and intelligent systems.

---

## Principle 6 — Continuous Evolution

ACKIOS itself must remain an evolving system.

The platform should continuously improve its ability to understand, assist, and support software creation.

---

## Final Founder Statement

ACKIOS is built as a long-term intelligence platform.

Every component should contribute toward creating a system that helps humans build, understand, and evolve complex digital worlds.

# ACKIOS Private Architecture

## 1. Architecture Separation Model

ACKIOS follows a dual-layer architecture model:

Open Foundation + Private Core.

This separation allows ACKIOS to grow as an ecosystem while protecting the strategic intelligence layer.

---

## 2. Open Foundation Layer

The Open Foundation represents the publicly accessible development layer.

Responsibilities:

- developer tooling;
- command-line interfaces;
- architecture scanning;
- reporting;
- plugin interfaces;
- integrations;
- community extensions.

The purpose of the Open Foundation is adoption, usability, and ecosystem growth.

---

## 3. Private Core Layer

The Private Core represents the protected intelligence layer of ACKIOS.

Responsibilities:

- advanced system understanding;
- cognitive processing;
- knowledge intelligence;
- reasoning capabilities;
- planning mechanisms;
- autonomous agent coordination;
- future evolution systems.

The Private Core creates the unique identity of ACKIOS.

---

## 4. Core Separation Principle

Public components provide capabilities.

Private components provide differentiation.

The boundary between these layers must remain intentional and protected.


---

## 5. Internal Architecture Flow

ACKIOS internal architecture follows a layered intelligence pipeline.

The system evolves from understanding toward intelligent assistance.

---

## Layer 1 — Project Understanding

Input:

- source code;
- project structure;
- dependencies;
- documentation;
- development history.

The purpose of this layer is to create a complete project representation.

---

## Layer 2 — Architecture Intelligence

Responsibilities:

- structural analysis;
- dependency analysis;
- architecture validation;
- system mapping.

This layer transforms raw project data into architectural knowledge.

---

## Layer 3 — Knowledge Representation

Responsibilities:

- knowledge graph creation;
- relationship tracking;
- architectural memory;
- decision history.

The goal is to maintain a persistent understanding of the project.

---

## Layer 4 — Brain Kernel

The Brain Kernel provides central intelligence capabilities.

Responsibilities:

- context management;
- knowledge processing;
- reasoning preparation;
- intelligence coordination.

---

## Layer 5 — Cognitive Runtime

The Cognitive Runtime enables advanced processes:

- reasoning;
- planning;
- reflection;
- evaluation;
- decision support.

---

## Layer 6 — Agent Execution

Agents operate on top of the intelligence foundation.

They provide:

- specialized analysis;
- recommendations;
- improvement proposals;
- controlled actions.

---

## Layer 7 — Evolution Engine

The final layer enables continuous improvement.

ACKIOS should help systems evolve safely through accumulated knowledge and intelligent guidance.


---

## 6. Knowledge Intelligence Layer

The Knowledge Intelligence Layer is responsible for transforming project information into persistent knowledge.

Traditional tools analyze current files.

ACKIOS should maintain an evolving understanding of the complete system.

---

## Knowledge Graph Foundation

The Knowledge Graph represents the internal model of a software system.

It contains relationships between:

- components;
- modules;
- dependencies;
- architectural boundaries;
- decisions;
- behaviors;
- historical changes.

---

## Knowledge Model

ACKIOS knowledge should represent:

- what exists;
- how components interact;
- why decisions were made;
- how the system evolved;
- what changes may affect the future.

---

## Architectural Memory

The system should preserve important knowledge over time.

Architectural memory includes:

- design decisions;
- previous changes;
- discovered patterns;
- known risks;
- improvement history.

---

## Knowledge Evolution

The Knowledge Intelligence Layer must continuously improve.

Every analysis cycle should increase ACKIOS understanding of the project.

The objective is to create a persistent intelligence model rather than temporary analysis results.


---

## 7. Brain Kernel Architecture

The Brain Kernel is the central intelligence foundation of ACKIOS.

It is responsible for transforming stored knowledge into intelligent understanding.

The Brain Kernel is not a simple processing module.

It represents the internal intelligence coordination layer of the system.

---

## Brain Kernel Responsibilities

The Brain Kernel manages:

- knowledge processing;
- context awareness;
- reasoning preparation;
- intelligence coordination;
- memory interaction;
- system understanding.

---

## Internal Brain Components

The Brain Kernel consists of multiple conceptual subsystems:

### Context Engine

Responsible for maintaining awareness of:

- current project state;
- user objectives;
- system environment;
- historical context.

---

### Knowledge Processing Engine

Responsible for:

- interpreting knowledge;
- connecting relationships;
- identifying patterns;
- preparing information for reasoning.

---

### Memory Integration Layer

Responsible for:

- storing important knowledge;
- retrieving previous decisions;
- maintaining continuity over time.

---

### Intelligence Coordination Layer

Responsible for connecting:

- Knowledge Graph;
- Cognitive Runtime;
- Agents;
- Recommendation Systems.

---

## Brain Kernel Principle

The Brain Kernel should not replace human decision making.

Its purpose is to provide deeper understanding and intelligent assistance.

The Brain Kernel becomes the foundation that allows ACKIOS to evolve from a toolkit into an intelligent software partner.


---

## 8. Cognitive Runtime Integration

The Cognitive Runtime is the intelligence execution environment of ACKIOS.

It transforms stored knowledge and context into cognitive processes.

The Cognitive Runtime operates on top of the Brain Kernel.

---

## Cognitive Runtime Responsibilities

The Cognitive Runtime provides:

- reasoning;
- planning;
- reflection;
- evaluation;
- decision support.

Its purpose is to transform understanding into intelligent actions.

---

## Reasoning System

The Reasoning System evaluates information and relationships.

Responsibilities:

- identify patterns;
- analyze consequences;
- compare possible solutions;
- generate intelligent conclusions.

---

## Planning System

The Planning System transforms goals into structured actions.

Responsibilities:

- create execution plans;
- evaluate dependencies;
- predict possible outcomes;
- coordinate future steps.

---

## Reflection System

The Reflection System allows ACKIOS to evaluate its own processes.

Responsibilities:

- analyze previous results;
- detect mistakes;
- improve recommendations;
- increase system effectiveness.

---

## Decision Support System

The Decision Support System helps humans make better engineering decisions.

It provides:

- recommendations;
- risk analysis;
- architectural suggestions;
- improvement opportunities.

---

## Cognitive Runtime Principle

The Cognitive Runtime should operate as an intelligence layer between knowledge and action.

It enables ACKIOS to move from passive system analysis toward active intelligent collaboration.


---

## 9. Agent System Boundary

The Agent System represents the execution layer of ACKIOS intelligence.

Agents operate on top of the Brain Kernel and Cognitive Runtime.

Agents are not independent intelligence systems.

They are specialized capabilities connected to the central intelligence architecture.

---

## Agent Architecture Principles

Every ACKIOS agent must:

- have a defined responsibility;
- operate within permissions;
- use shared knowledge;
- respect architecture boundaries;
- provide explainable results.

---

## Agent Types

Future ACKIOS agents may include:

### Architecture Agent

Responsibilities:

- analyze architecture;
- detect structural problems;
- suggest improvements.

---

### Development Agent

Responsibilities:

- assist implementation;
- analyze changes;
- suggest engineering solutions.

---

### Documentation Agent

Responsibilities:

- maintain project knowledge;
- generate technical documentation;
- preserve system understanding.

---

### Security Agent

Responsibilities:

- identify risks;
- analyze vulnerabilities;
- recommend protection strategies.

---

### Evolution Agent

Responsibilities:

- analyze long-term improvement opportunities;
- evaluate system growth;
- propose evolution paths.

---

## Agent Coordination

Agents communicate through controlled intelligence interfaces.

The Brain Kernel coordinates:

- context;
- knowledge;
- priorities;
- results.

The goal is not autonomous replacement of engineers.

The goal is intelligent collaboration between humans and specialized software agents.

---

## Agent Boundary Principle

Agents provide specialized intelligence.

The Brain Kernel provides shared understanding.

The Cognitive Runtime provides reasoning.

Together they create the foundation for future ACKIOS autonomous capabilities.


---

## 10. Future Expansion Rules

ACKIOS must evolve while preserving architectural integrity.

Future expansion must follow controlled principles.

---

## Rule 1 — Preserve Layer Boundaries

New capabilities must belong to a clearly defined architectural layer.

A new feature must not break the separation between:

- Open Foundation;
- Private Core;
- Intelligence Layers;
- Execution Systems.

---

## Rule 2 — Expand Through Architecture

New functionality should be introduced through stable architectural concepts.

Expansion should prefer:

- new modules;
- new intelligence capabilities;
- new controlled interfaces.

Avoid isolated feature growth without architectural purpose.

---

## Rule 3 — Knowledge First

Every major system extension should increase ACKIOS understanding.

New components should contribute to:

- knowledge;
- memory;
- reasoning;
- system evolution.

---

## Rule 4 — Maintain Explainability

Future intelligent capabilities must provide understandable results.

ACKIOS should explain:

- what it detected;
- why it made a recommendation;
- how conclusions were reached.

---

## Rule 5 — Protect Core Differentiation

Public expansion should grow the ecosystem.

Private development should preserve strategic intelligence advantages.

---

## Rule 6 — Evolution Over Accumulation

ACKIOS should not become a collection of disconnected features.

Every addition must support the long-term vision of an intelligent software evolution platform.

---

## Final Architecture Principle

ACKIOS architecture must evolve like an intelligent system:

- stable foundations;
- adaptive capabilities;
- accumulated knowledge;
- controlled evolution.

The architecture itself becomes a living framework for future intelligence.

import { BrainKernelConfig } from "./BrainKernelConfig.js";
import { IBrainKernel } from "./IBrainKernel.js";

import { BrainServiceRegistry } from "../services/BrainServiceRegistry.js";
import { BrainState } from "../state/BrainState.js";

import { MemoryManager } from "../memory/MemoryManager.js";
import { KnowledgeManager } from "../knowledge/KnowledgeManager.js";
import { ContextManager } from "../context/ContextManager.js";
import { ReasoningManager } from "../reasoning/ReasoningManager.js";
import { PlanningManager } from "../planning/PlanningManager.js";
import { DecisionManager } from "../decision/DecisionManager.js";
import { LearningManager } from "../learning/LearningManager.js";
import { ReflectionManager } from "../reflection/ReflectionManager.js";
import { PredictionManager } from "../prediction/PredictionManager.js";
import { ExecutionManager } from "../execution/ExecutionManager.js";
import { AgentManager } from "../agents/AgentManager.js";
import { ToolManager } from "../tools/ToolManager.js";
import { EventManager } from "../events/EventManager.js";
import { TelemetryManager } from "../telemetry/TelemetryManager.js";
import { KnowledgePersistenceService } from "../storage/KnowledgePersistenceService.js";

export interface BrainKernelServices {

    memory: MemoryManager;

    knowledge: KnowledgeManager;

    context: ContextManager;

    reasoning: ReasoningManager;

    planning: PlanningManager;

    decision: DecisionManager;

    learning: LearningManager;

    reflection: ReflectionManager;

    prediction: PredictionManager;

    execution: ExecutionManager;

    agents: AgentManager;

    tools: ToolManager;

    events: EventManager;

    telemetry: TelemetryManager;

    persistence?: KnowledgePersistenceService;

}

export class BrainKernel implements IBrainKernel {

    public readonly services = new BrainServiceRegistry();

    public readonly state = new BrainState();

    public readonly memory: MemoryManager;

    public readonly knowledge: KnowledgeManager;

    public readonly context: ContextManager;

    public readonly reasoning: ReasoningManager;

    public readonly planning: PlanningManager;

    public readonly decision: DecisionManager;

    public readonly learning: LearningManager;

    public readonly reflection: ReflectionManager;

    public readonly prediction: PredictionManager;

    public readonly execution: ExecutionManager;

    public readonly agents: AgentManager;

    public readonly tools: ToolManager;

    public readonly events: EventManager;

    public readonly telemetry: TelemetryManager;

    public constructor(
        services: BrainKernelServices,
        public readonly config: BrainKernelConfig = {}
    ){

        this.memory = services.memory;
        this.knowledge = services.knowledge;
        this.context = services.context;
        this.reasoning = services.reasoning;
        this.planning = services.planning;
        this.decision = services.decision;
        this.learning = services.learning;
        this.reflection = services.reflection;
        this.prediction = services.prediction;
        this.execution = services.execution;
        this.agents = services.agents;
        this.tools = services.tools;
        this.events = services.events;
        this.telemetry = services.telemetry;


        [
            this.memory,
            this.knowledge,
            this.context,
            this.reasoning,
            this.planning,
            this.decision,
            this.learning,
            this.reflection,
            this.prediction,
            this.execution,
            this.agents,
            this.tools,
            this.events,
            this.telemetry
        ].forEach(service => this.services.register(service));

    }


    public async initialize(): Promise<void> {

        await this.services.initialize();

        this.state.initialized = true;

    }


    public async start(): Promise<void> {

        this.state.running = true;

    }


    public async stop(): Promise<void> {

        this.state.running = false;

    }


    public async dispose(): Promise<void> {

        await this.services.dispose();

        this.state.disposed = true;

    }

}

============================================================
KERNEL BUILDER
============================================================
import { BrainKernel } from "./BrainKernel.js";
import { BrainKernelConfig } from "./BrainKernelConfig.js";

import { MemoryManager } from "../memory/MemoryManager.js";
import { KnowledgeManager } from "../knowledge/KnowledgeManager.js";
import { ContextManager } from "../context/ContextManager.js";
import { ReasoningManager } from "../reasoning/ReasoningManager.js";
import { PlanningManager } from "../planning/PlanningManager.js";
import { DecisionManager } from "../decision/DecisionManager.js";
import { LearningManager } from "../learning/LearningManager.js";
import { ReflectionManager } from "../reflection/ReflectionManager.js";
import { PredictionManager } from "../prediction/PredictionManager.js";
import { ExecutionManager } from "../execution/ExecutionManager.js";
import { AgentManager } from "../agents/AgentManager.js";
import { ToolManager } from "../tools/ToolManager.js";
import { EventManager } from "../events/EventManager.js";
import { TelemetryManager } from "../telemetry/TelemetryManager.js";

import { BrainKnowledgeExporter } from "../storage/BrainKnowledgeExporter.js";
import { BrainKnowledgeImporter } from "../storage/BrainKnowledgeImporter.js";
import { KnowledgePersistenceService } from "../storage/KnowledgePersistenceService.js";

export class BrainKernelBuilder {

    public build(
        config: BrainKernelConfig = {}
    ): BrainKernel {

        const memory = new MemoryManager();
        const knowledge = new KnowledgeManager();
        const context = new ContextManager();
        const reasoning = new ReasoningManager();
        const planning = new PlanningManager();
        const decision = new DecisionManager();
        const learning = new LearningManager();
        const reflection = new ReflectionManager();
        const prediction = new PredictionManager();
        const execution = new ExecutionManager();
        const agents = new AgentManager();
        const tools = new ToolManager();
        const events = new EventManager();
        const telemetry = new TelemetryManager();

        const exporter = new BrainKnowledgeExporter();
        const importer = new BrainKnowledgeImporter();

        const persistence = config.storage
            ? new KnowledgePersistenceService(
                knowledge,
                exporter,
                importer,
                config.storage
            )
            : undefined;


        return new BrainKernel(

            {
                memory,
                knowledge,
                context,
                reasoning,
                planning,
                decision,
                learning,
                reflection,
                prediction,
                execution,
                agents,
                tools,
                events,
                telemetry,
                persistence
            },

            config

        );

    }

}

============================================================
BRAIN RUNTIME BUILDER
============================================================
import { BrainRuntime } from "./BrainRuntime.js";
import { BrainPipeline } from "../pipeline/BrainPipeline.js";
import { BrainDispatcher } from "../dispatcher/BrainDispatcher.js";
import { BrainScheduler } from "../scheduler/BrainScheduler.js";
import { BrainExecutor } from "../executor/BrainExecutor.js";

export class BrainRuntimeBuilder {

    public build(): BrainRuntime {

        const pipeline = new BrainPipeline();

        const dispatcher = new BrainDispatcher();

        const scheduler = new BrainScheduler();

        const executor = new BrainExecutor();

        return new BrainRuntime(
            pipeline,
            dispatcher,
            scheduler,
            executor
        );

    }

}

============================================================
COGNITIVE RUNTIME BUILDER
============================================================
import { CognitiveRuntime } from "../runtime/CognitiveRuntime.js";
import { CognitivePipeline } from "../pipeline/CognitivePipeline.js";

export class CognitiveRuntimeBuilder {

    public build(): CognitiveRuntime {

        const pipeline = new CognitivePipeline();

        return new CognitiveRuntime(
            pipeline
        );

    }

}

============================================================
TOOLKIT
============================================================
import { AnalysisEngineBuilder } from "./engine/AnalysisEngineBuilder.js";
import { ProjectScanner } from "./scanner/ProjectScanner.js";
import { RepositoryLocator } from "./scanner/RepositoryLocator.js";

import { DefaultWorkspaceLoader } from "./workspace/loader/DefaultWorkspaceLoader.js";
import { DefaultWorkspaceManager } from "./workspace/manager/DefaultWorkspaceManager.js";

export class Toolkit {

    private readonly workspace =
        new DefaultWorkspaceManager(
            new DefaultWorkspaceLoader(
                new ProjectScanner()
            ),
            new RepositoryLocator()
        );

    private readonly analysis =
        new AnalysisEngineBuilder().build();

    public async analyze(
        root: string
    ) {

        const workspace =
            await this.workspace.open({
                root
            });

        return this.analysis.analyze(
            workspace
        );

    }

}

src\brain\core\BrainKernel.ts

import { BrainKernelConfig } from "./BrainKernelConfig.js";
import { IBrainKernel } from "./IBrainKernel.js";

import { BrainServiceRegistry } from "../services/BrainServiceRegistry.js";
import { BrainState } from "../state/BrainState.js";

import { MemoryManager } from "../memory/MemoryManager.js";
import { KnowledgeManager } from "../knowledge/KnowledgeManager.js";
import { ContextManager } from "../context/ContextManager.js";
import { ReasoningManager } from "../reasoning/ReasoningManager.js";
import { PlanningManager } from "../planning/PlanningManager.js";
import { DecisionManager } from "../decision/DecisionManager.js";
import { LearningManager } from "../learning/LearningManager.js";
import { ReflectionManager } from "../reflection/ReflectionManager.js";
import { PredictionManager } from "../prediction/PredictionManager.js";
import { ExecutionManager } from "../execution/ExecutionManager.js";
import { AgentManager } from "../agents/AgentManager.js";
import { ToolManager } from "../tools/ToolManager.js";
import { EventManager } from "../events/EventManager.js";
import { TelemetryManager } from "../telemetry/TelemetryManager.js";
import { KnowledgePersistenceService } from "../storage/KnowledgePersistenceService.js";

export interface BrainKernelServices {

    memory: MemoryManager;

    knowledge: KnowledgeManager;

    context: ContextManager;

    reasoning: ReasoningManager;

    planning: PlanningManager;

    decision: DecisionManager;

    learning: LearningManager;

    reflection: ReflectionManager;

    prediction: PredictionManager;

    execution: ExecutionManager;

    agents: AgentManager;

    tools: ToolManager;

    events: EventManager;

    telemetry: TelemetryManager;

    persistence?: KnowledgePersistenceService;

}

export class BrainKernel implements IBrainKernel {

    public readonly services = new BrainServiceRegistry();

    public readonly state = new BrainState();

    public readonly memory: MemoryManager;

    public readonly knowledge: KnowledgeManager;

    public readonly context: ContextManager;

    public readonly reasoning: ReasoningManager;

    public readonly planning: PlanningManager;

    public readonly decision: DecisionManager;

    public readonly learning: LearningManager;

    public readonly reflection: ReflectionManager;

    public readonly prediction: PredictionManager;

    public readonly execution: ExecutionManager;

    public readonly agents: AgentManager;

    public readonly tools: ToolManager;

    public readonly events: EventManager;

    public readonly telemetry: TelemetryManager;

    public constructor(
        services: BrainKernelServices,
        public readonly config: BrainKernelConfig = {}
    ){

        this.memory = services.memory;
        this.knowledge = services.knowledge;
        this.context = services.context;
        this.reasoning = services.reasoning;
        this.planning = services.planning;
        this.decision = services.decision;
        this.learning = services.learning;
        this.reflection = services.reflection;
        this.prediction = services.prediction;
        this.execution = services.execution;
        this.agents = services.agents;
        this.tools = services.tools;
        this.events = services.events;
        this.telemetry = services.telemetry;


        [
            this.memory,
            this.knowledge,
            this.context,
            this.reasoning,
            this.planning,
            this.decision,
            this.learning,
            this.reflection,
            this.prediction,
            this.execution,
            this.agents,
            this.tools,
            this.events,
            this.telemetry
        ].forEach(service => this.services.register(service));

    }


    public async initialize(): Promise<void> {

        await this.services.initialize();

        this.state.initialized = true;

    }


    public async start(): Promise<void> {

        this.state.running = true;

    }


    public async stop(): Promise<void> {

        this.state.running = false;

    }


    public async dispose(): Promise<void> {

        await this.services.dispose();

        this.state.disposed = true;

    }

}

============================================================
src\brain\core\BrainKernelBuilder.ts
============================================================

import { BrainKernel } from "./BrainKernel.js";
import { BrainKernelConfig } from "./BrainKernelConfig.js";

import { MemoryManager } from "../memory/MemoryManager.js";
import { KnowledgeManager } from "../knowledge/KnowledgeManager.js";
import { ContextManager } from "../context/ContextManager.js";
import { ReasoningManager } from "../reasoning/ReasoningManager.js";
import { PlanningManager } from "../planning/PlanningManager.js";
import { DecisionManager } from "../decision/DecisionManager.js";
import { LearningManager } from "../learning/LearningManager.js";
import { ReflectionManager } from "../reflection/ReflectionManager.js";
import { PredictionManager } from "../prediction/PredictionManager.js";
import { ExecutionManager } from "../execution/ExecutionManager.js";
import { AgentManager } from "../agents/AgentManager.js";
import { ToolManager } from "../tools/ToolManager.js";
import { EventManager } from "../events/EventManager.js";
import { TelemetryManager } from "../telemetry/TelemetryManager.js";

import { BrainKnowledgeExporter } from "../storage/BrainKnowledgeExporter.js";
import { BrainKnowledgeImporter } from "../storage/BrainKnowledgeImporter.js";
import { KnowledgePersistenceService } from "../storage/KnowledgePersistenceService.js";

export class BrainKernelBuilder {

    public build(
        config: BrainKernelConfig = {}
    ): BrainKernel {

        const memory = new MemoryManager();
        const knowledge = new KnowledgeManager();
        const context = new ContextManager();
        const reasoning = new ReasoningManager();
        const planning = new PlanningManager();
        const decision = new DecisionManager();
        const learning = new LearningManager();
        const reflection = new ReflectionManager();
        const prediction = new PredictionManager();
        const execution = new ExecutionManager();
        const agents = new AgentManager();
        const tools = new ToolManager();
        const events = new EventManager();
        const telemetry = new TelemetryManager();

        const exporter = new BrainKnowledgeExporter();
        const importer = new BrainKnowledgeImporter();

        const persistence = config.storage
            ? new KnowledgePersistenceService(
                knowledge,
                exporter,
                importer,
                config.storage
            )
            : undefined;


        return new BrainKernel(

            {
                memory,
                knowledge,
                context,
                reasoning,
                planning,
                decision,
                learning,
                reflection,
                prediction,
                execution,
                agents,
                tools,
                events,
                telemetry,
                persistence
            },

            config

        );

    }

}

============================================================
src\brain\runtime\host\BrainRuntime.ts
============================================================

import { BrainDispatcher } from "../dispatcher/BrainDispatcher.js";
import { BrainExecutor } from "../executor/BrainExecutor.js";
import { BrainPipeline } from "../pipeline/BrainPipeline.js";
import { BrainScheduler } from "../scheduler/BrainScheduler.js";
import { RuntimeContext } from "../context/RuntimeContext.js";

import { MemoryStage } from "../stages/MemoryStage.js";
import { KnowledgeStage } from "../stages/KnowledgeStage.js";
import { ReasoningStage } from "../stages/ReasoningStage.js";
import { PlanningStage } from "../stages/PlanningStage.js";
import { DecisionStage } from "../stages/DecisionStage.js";
import { ExecutionStage } from "../stages/ExecutionStage.js";

import { IBrainRuntime } from "./IBrainRuntime.js";

export class BrainRuntime implements IBrainRuntime {

    private running = false;

    public constructor(
        private readonly pipeline: BrainPipeline,
        private readonly dispatcher: BrainDispatcher,
        private readonly scheduler: BrainScheduler,
        private readonly executor: BrainExecutor
    ) {

        this.pipeline
            .addStage(new MemoryStage())
            .addStage(new KnowledgeStage())
            .addStage(new ReasoningStage())
            .addStage(new PlanningStage())
            .addStage(new DecisionStage())
            .addStage(new ExecutionStage());

    }

    public async start(): Promise<void> {

        this.running = true;

    }

    public async stop(): Promise<void> {

        this.running = false;

    }

    public getPipeline(): BrainPipeline {

        return this.pipeline;

    }

    public async run(
        context: RuntimeContext
    ): Promise<RuntimeContext> {

        if (!this.running) {
            await this.start();
        }

        return await this.dispatcher.dispatch(async () => {

            let current = context;

            this.scheduler.enqueue(async () => {

                current = await this.pipeline.execute(current);

            });

            await this.scheduler.run();

            await this.executor.execute(async () => {});

            return current;

        });

    }

}

============================================================
src\brain\runtime\host\BrainRuntimeBuilder.ts
============================================================

import { BrainRuntime } from "./BrainRuntime.js";
import { BrainPipeline } from "../pipeline/BrainPipeline.js";
import { BrainDispatcher } from "../dispatcher/BrainDispatcher.js";
import { BrainScheduler } from "../scheduler/BrainScheduler.js";
import { BrainExecutor } from "../executor/BrainExecutor.js";

export class BrainRuntimeBuilder {

    public build(): BrainRuntime {

        const pipeline = new BrainPipeline();

        const dispatcher = new BrainDispatcher();

        const scheduler = new BrainScheduler();

        const executor = new BrainExecutor();

        return new BrainRuntime(
            pipeline,
            dispatcher,
            scheduler,
            executor
        );

    }

}

============================================================
src\brain\cognition\factory\CognitiveRuntimeBuilder.ts
============================================================

import { CognitiveRuntime } from "../runtime/CognitiveRuntime.js";
import { CognitivePipeline } from "../pipeline/CognitivePipeline.js";

export class CognitiveRuntimeBuilder {

    public build(): CognitiveRuntime {

        const pipeline = new CognitivePipeline();

        return new CognitiveRuntime(
            pipeline
        );

    }

}

D:\ACKIOS\toolkit\docs\architecture\compliance\ACS.md
# Architecture Compliance Scanner (ACS)

## Purpose

Architecture Compliance Scanner (ACS) validates that a project conforms to the architectural standards defined by ACKI OS.

ACS is intended to automate architectural governance and prevent architectural drift.

## Responsibilities

ACS is responsible for:

- Scanning repository structure.
- Building an architecture model.
- Building a dependency graph.
- Loading project source files.
- Validating architecture rules.
- Detecting violations.
- Producing architecture reports.

## Planned Pipeline

1. ArchitectureScanner
2. ArchitectureParser
3. GraphBuilder
4. SourceLoader
5. Rule Engine
6. Validator
7. Report Generator

## Design Principles

- Read-only analysis.
- No source code modification.
- Deterministic results.
- Extensible rule system.
- Dependency Injection friendly.
- Fully testable components.

## Expected Outputs

ACS produces:

- Architecture Report
- Dependency Graph
- Rule Violations
- Compliance Score
- Recommendations

## Future Extensions

Future versions may include:

- Import resolution
- Circular dependency detection
- Layer validation
- Module boundary validation
- Architecture trend analysis
- CI/CD integration

########################################
D:\ACKIOS\toolkit\docs\architecture\laws\LAW-001_DEPENDENCY_DIRECTION.md
# LAW-001 — Dependency Direction

Version: 1.0

Status: Draft

---

# Statement

Dependencies SHALL point only from higher architectural layers to lower architectural layers.

Lower architectural layers MUST NOT depend on higher architectural layers.

---

# Purpose

This law preserves architectural stability, prevents cyclic dependencies, and enables independent evolution of subsystems.

---

# Rules

1. BrainKernel MAY depend on infrastructure services.

2. Runtime MAY depend on contracts and services.

3. Pipeline MAY depend on Stage contracts.

4. Stage MAY depend on Context, Contracts, and Services.

5. Managers MAY depend on Engines and domain models.

6. Engines MUST NOT depend on Runtime, Pipeline, or Stages.

7. Lower layers MUST NEVER reference higher layers.

---

# Examples

Valid:

BrainRuntime
    ↓
BrainPipeline
    ↓
RuntimeStage
    ↓
Manager
    ↓
Engine

Invalid:

Engine
    ↓
Runtime

Manager
    ↓
Pipeline

Stage
    ↓
BrainRuntime

---

# Compliance

Every pull request introducing a new dependency SHALL be evaluated against this law.

Any violation MUST be documented through an ADR before implementation.

---

# Rationale

Stable dependency direction allows ACKI OS to evolve incrementally without creating architectural cycles or hidden coupling.

########################################
D:\ACKIOS\toolkit\docs\architecture\process\ARCHITECTURE_DEVELOPMENT_LIFECYCLE.md

########################################
D:\ACKIOS\toolkit\docs\architecture\process\CHANGE_PROCESS.md
# Architecture Change Process

## Purpose

This document defines the standard process for introducing architectural changes in ACKI OS.

## Development Workflow

Every architectural change follows the same lifecycle:

1. Analyze the current implementation.
2. Design the minimal required change.
3. Implement the change.
4. Execute all relevant tests.
5. Review architecture impact.
6. Update documentation if required.
7. Commit using an atomic commit.
8. Merge only after successful validation.

## Rules

- Avoid large refactorings.
- Keep commits small and focused.
- Preserve backward compatibility whenever possible.
- Introduce new abstractions only when justified.
- Every new subsystem should include tests.

## Validation Checklist

- Code compiles.
- Tests pass.
- Dependency Injection is preserved.
- Documentation is updated.
- Architecture remains consistent.

D:\ACKIOS\toolkit\docs\architecture\reference\types\Manager.md
# AAL Type: Manager

Version: 1.0

Status: Draft

---

# Purpose

A Manager owns a single domain of responsibility within ACKI OS.

A Manager coordinates domain operations but does not orchestrate system execution.

---

# Contract

A Manager MUST:

- own exactly one domain;
- expose the public API of its domain;
- preserve domain consistency;
- delegate specialized processing to supporting components when appropriate.

A Manager MUST NOT:

- control Runtime execution;
- control Pipeline execution;
- own responsibilities of another Manager;
- contain presentation or infrastructure concerns.

---

# Responsibilities

A Manager is responsible for:

- domain coordination;
- domain state management;
- domain validation;
- interaction with supporting Engines and Stores.

---

# Dependencies

A Manager MAY depend on:

- Engines
- Stores
- Domain Models
- Contracts

A Manager MUST NOT depend on:

- Runtime
- Pipeline
- Stages
- other Managers unless explicitly defined by architecture

---

# Lifecycle

Initialize
      │
      ▼
Serve Requests
      │
      ▼
Dispose

---

# Extension Rules

New Managers SHALL own exactly one domain.

Domain boundaries MUST remain explicit.

---

# Compliance Checklist

A Manager:

☐ Owns one domain

☐ Exposes domain operations

☐ Does not orchestrate Runtime

☐ Does not control Pipeline

☐ Delegates specialized processing

☐ Is independently testable

########################################
D:\ACKIOS\toolkit\docs\architecture\reference\types\Stage.md
# AAL Type: Stage

Version: 1.0

Status: Draft

---

# Purpose

A Stage represents a single, ordered step of cognitive execution within a Pipeline.

A Stage SHALL perform exactly one architectural responsibility.

---

# Contract

A Stage MUST:

- receive an input context;
- produce an output context;
- preserve pipeline continuity;
- remain independently testable.

A Stage MUST NOT:

- own domain state;
- control pipeline execution;
- instantiate infrastructure services;
- perform responsibilities of another Stage.

---

# Responsibilities

A Stage is responsible for:

- transforming context;
- invoking the appropriate domain service when required;
- preparing context for the next Stage.

---

# Dependencies

A Stage MAY depend on:

- Context
- Service interfaces
- Contracts

A Stage SHOULD NOT depend directly on:

- other Stages
- Pipeline implementation
- Runtime implementation

---

# Side Effects

Side effects MUST be explicit.

Persistent changes SHOULD be delegated to Managers or Services.

---

# Lifecycle

Input Context
      │
      ▼
Stage Execution
      │
      ▼
Output Context

---

# Composition

Stages SHALL execute sequentially inside a Pipeline.

Stages MUST remain independent.

---

# Extension Rules

New Stages SHALL follow this contract.

Existing Stages SHOULD evolve without breaking the pipeline contract.

---

# Compliance Checklist

A Stage:

☐ Has one responsibility

☐ Receives Context

☐ Produces Context

☐ Does not own business state

☐ Is independently testable

☐ Can be reordered only through Pipeline configuration

########################################
D:\ACKIOS\toolkit\docs\architecture\reference\ARCHITECTURE_LANGUAGE.md
# ACKI Architecture Language (AAL)

Version: 1.0

Status: Draft

---

# Purpose

ACKI Architecture Language (AAL) defines the architectural vocabulary of ACKI OS.

Every architectural component SHALL conform to one of the definitions described in this document.

This document defines concepts rather than implementations.

---

# Architectural Types

The following architectural types are currently recognized:

- Kernel
- Runtime
- Pipeline
- Stage
- Manager
- Engine
- Builder
- Bridge
- Registry
- Service
- Context
- Module
- Factory

---

# Evolution

New architectural types MAY be introduced.

Existing architectural types MUST remain backward compatible whenever possible.

---

# Compliance

Every new architectural component SHALL be classified using one of the architectural types defined by AAL.

If no suitable architectural type exists, the architecture SHALL be extended before implementation begins.

D:\ACKIOS\toolkit\docs\architecture\runtime\00_RUNTIME_OVERVIEW.md
# ACKI OS Runtime Overview

## Purpose

Brain Runtime is the execution engine of ACKI OS.

Its responsibility is to execute the cognitive pipeline using services provided by the Brain Kernel while remaining independent from cognitive implementation details.

---

## Runtime Architecture

BrainKernelBuilder
        │
        ▼
BrainKernel
        │
        ▼
BrainServiceRegistry
        │
        ▼
BrainRuntimeBridge
        │
        ├────────────────────────────┐
        ▼                            ▼
BrainRuntime                CognitiveRuntime
        │
        ▼
BrainPipeline
        │
        ▼
MemoryStage
KnowledgeStage
ReasoningStage
PlanningStage
DecisionStage
ExecutionStage

---

## Responsibilities

Brain Runtime is responsible for:

- Runtime execution
- Pipeline orchestration
- Stage execution
- Runtime lifecycle
- Runtime context propagation
- Service access through BrainServiceRegistry

Brain Runtime is NOT responsible for:

- reasoning implementation
- planning implementation
- knowledge processing
- memory storage implementation

Those responsibilities belong to Managers.

---

## Design Principles

1. Runtime never owns business logic.

2. Runtime only orchestrates execution.

3. Every RuntimeStage has a single responsibility.

4. Services are accessed only through BrainServiceRegistry.

5. Runtime is extensible without changing BrainKernel.

6. Runtime stages define the cognitive execution order.

---

## Cognitive Execution Pipeline

Memory
    ↓
Knowledge
    ↓
Reasoning
    ↓
Planning
    ↓
Decision
    ↓
Execution

This pipeline represents the canonical execution order inside ACKI OS.

Future versions may extend stage internals without changing the pipeline contract.

---

## Status

Architecture Status:
Stable

Sprint:
93

Runtime Foundation:
Completed

Architecture Freeze:
In Progress

• Dependency Injection

• Composition over inheritance
